payload.topic: why flaky tests erode team trust
payload.brief: a short, punchy opinion post for engineers

write.post: A test that fails 1 in 20 runs isn't a 5% problem, it's a 100% problem: every red build now needs a human to decide if it's real. After the third false alarm, people stop reading failures and start hitting rerun. Once a team ignores 30% of its red builds, the suite has stopped being a signal and become a tax.

input.topic: why flaky tests erode team trust
input.brief: a short, punchy opinion post for engineers
feedback: Zero to one numeric statistics; needs at least two distinct concrete numbers/percentages/units. 'three times' is the only number, and 'coin flip' is not numeric.
No real statistics (percentages, counts, units) — the standard requires concrete stats, not rhetorical figures.

output.post: A test that fails 1 in 20 runs isn't a 5% problem, it's a 100% problem: every red build now needs a human to decide if it's real. After the third false alarm, people stop reading failures and start hitting rerun. Once a team ignores 30% of its red builds, the suite has stopped being a signal and become a tax.

Flaky tests train engineers to hit "retry" instead of reading the failure. Once that reflex sets in, a real regression gets the same shrug as a timing blip, and the suite stops being a signal anyone acts on. A test that fails 1 in 20 runs isn't 95% reliable — across a 200-test suite it means most green builds passed by luck, not correctness. The fix isn't more retries; it's quarantining the flake and treating an unexplained failure as a bug, not noise.

Flaky tests train your team to ignore red builds. Once "just hit rerun" becomes the reflex, a real regression looks identical to noise and ships anyway. A suite you retry three times isn't a signal—it's a coin flip you've stopped reading.

A test that fails 1 in 20 runs isn't a 5% problem, it's a 100% problem: every red build now needs a human to decide if it's real. After the third false alarm, people stop reading failures and start hitting rerun. Once a team ignores 30% of its red builds, the suite has stopped being a signal and become a tax.
